Authors: Amir Memon

entry.title: SAP HANA System Replication Takeover and Client Connectivity
entry.content:
Documented Scope and Architectural Responsibilities


Introduction
This article summarizes publicly available SAP documentation and reflects the documented behavior of SAP HANA System Replication at the time of writing.

SAP HANA System Replication is widely used in productive landscapes to improve availability and to enable takeover to a secondary system.
In operational situations, however, a recurring observation can occur:

After a takeover, the secondary system assumes the primary database role, while application connectivity depends on the surrounding client access path.

This article does not provide configuration or implementation guidance.
Instead, it consolidates SAP documentation to clarify the documented scope of System Replication and the responsibilities described in related high-availability integration documentation.

The purpose is to align operational expectations with documented behavior.


Documented Purpose of System Replication
According to SAP documentation, SAP HANA System Replication continuously transfers database changes from a primary system to a secondary system and supports a role change (takeover).

SAP documentation describes that System Replication:

  • replicates database persistence (data and log changes)
  • enables takeover to a secondary system
  • preserves transactional consistency

Sources
SAP HANA System Replication Guide
https://help.sap.com/doc/c81e9406d08046c0a118c8bef71f6bdc/2.0.07/en-US/SAP_HANA_System_Replication_Guide_en.pdf
SAP Help Portal – System Replication Overview
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_HANA_PLATFORM/6b94445c94ae495c83a19646e7c3fd56/676844172c2442f0bf6c8b080db05ae7.html

After takeover, the secondary system becomes the new primary database system from the database perspective.
The documentation therefore describes System Replication primarily in terms of database availability and data consistency.


Documented Behavior During Takeover
During a takeover, SAP HANA changes the database role of the participating systems and starts the required database services on the new primary host.

From the database perspective described in the System Replication documentation, the takeover is considered completed once the primary role is active and database services are operational on the new primary host according to the System Replication documentation.

The System Replication Guide documents the database role change and service startup behavior.
The documentation describes database state and service availability, while network routing and client access handling are described separately in high-availability integration documentation.


Actions After Takeover in HA Integration Documentation
SAP documentation describes integration mechanisms that allow external components to react to a role change.

SAP HANA provides a HA/DR provider hook that enables external high-availability components to execute actions after a takeover.

Documented examples include service endpoint switching or cluster-related actions.

Sources
Implementing SAP HANA HA/DR Providers
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_HANA_PLATFORM/6b94445c94ae495c83a19646e7c3fd56/1200ab8ef0c54c54be2d0e7f5327f7ed.html
Installing and Configuring HA/DR Provider Hook
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_HANA_PLATFORM/6b94445c94ae495c83a19646e7c3fd56/2962efcfdd6740689d5705bdabe9a2d5.html

The HA/DR integration documentation describes coordination with external high-availability mechanisms following a database role change.


Database Role and Client Connectivity in Documented Architecture
System Replication changes the database role.
Client connectivity depends on the access path used by the application.

Typical productive environments use infrastructure components such as:

  • virtual IP addresses
  • cluster frameworks
  • load balancers
  • DNS-based host names

SAP System Replication documentation describes the database role and persistence synchronization, while HA integration documentation describes coordination with external availability mechanisms.
These documents therefore address different parts of the overall availability architecture.


Interpreting Connection Failures After Takeover
In operational situations, a takeover may be completed from the database perspective while application connections still fail if the client access path still targets the former primary host or has not yet been updated by the surrounding infrastructure.

From a documentation perspective, this reflects different layers of the availability architecture rather than a single mechanism.

SAP documentation separates:

  • database role change (System Replication)
  • external availability coordination (HA integration)

Reference
SAP Note 1999880 - FAQ: SAP HANA System Replication


Relationship to High Availability Architecture
SAP HANA high availability consists of multiple documented components, including:

  • System Replication
  • cluster or HA integration
  • infrastructure routing mechanisms

System Replication addresses database availability.
Other components coordinate access paths and service endpoints in the surrounding infrastructure.


Conclusion
SAP HANA System Replication provides database availability and transactional consistency after a takeover as described in the official documentation.

Client connectivity depends on surrounding high-availability and infrastructure mechanisms that are documented separately from System Replication.

Understanding the documented scope of these components helps distinguish database state from client access routing and supports clearer troubleshooting during incidents.

For implementation or configuration guidance, always follow the official SAP documentation and SAP recommendations.


Disclaimer
This article summarizes publicly available SAP documentation and does not replace official SAP configuration, sizing, or implementation guidance. This article describes documented architectural behavior and is not an SAP support statement.

entry.title: Avoiding HANA Connection Exhaustion: A Practical Guide to Hikari & Multi-Tenant Scaling
entry.content:
Introduction

Modern SAP applications running on SAP BTP with CAP Java and SAP HANA rely heavily on efficient database connection management.

Opening a new database connection for every request is expensive:

  • It increases latency
  • It consumes database resources
  • It reduces scalability

To solve this, we use connection pools.

In this blog, we’ll walk through how connection pooling works in CAP Java, what can go wrong in multi-tenant systems, and how to configure it correctly to avoid production outages.

What is a Connection Pool?

A connection pool is a cache of reusable database connections maintained by your application.

open connection → execute query → close connection

becomes:

borrow connection → execute query → return to pool

Benefits

  • Faster response times
  • Reduced database load
  • Reuse of expensive DB sessions
  • Better scalability

How CAP Java Uses Connection Pools

In CAP Java:

  • Database access uses JDBC connection pooling via Spring Boot
  • The default pool implementation is HikariCP
  • Each application instance maintains its own connection pool

This means that if your application is scaled horizontally, the number of connections increases with the number of instances.

Why This Becomes Critical in Production

In the basic mode, the HANA database is provisioned with 2 vCPUs, which allows roughly 1000 concurrent connections.

Now consider a typical CAP multi-tenant setup:

  • Multiple services
  • Multiple tenants
  • Multiple app instances (autoscaling)
  • Each instance maintaining its own pool

The total DB connections can grow very quickly if not controlled.

Multi-Tenant Scaling Problem

Without optimization, the connection count grows like this:

connections = instances × tenants × pool size

Example:

  • 4 tenants
  • 3 application instances
  • default pool size = 10
4 × 3 × 10 = 120 connections

Now multiply this across multiple services and sidecars, you can quickly reach the HANA limit.

The Solution: Shared Pools with combinePools

CAP Java provides a very important optimization:

cds:
  multi-tenancy:
    datasource:
      combinePools:
        enabled: true

This ensures that instead of creating pools per tenant, the application uses:

One shared connection pool per HANA database per application instance

Now the formula becomes:

connections = instances × pool size

This makes connection usage predictable and scalable.

HikariCP Configuration (Production)

A production-ready CAP Java configuration looks like this:

cds:
  multi-tenancy:
    datasource:
      combinePools:
        enabled: true
      hikari:
        maximum-pool-size: 40
        minimum-idle: 10
        idle-timeout: 600000
        max-lifetime: 3300000

Parameter Description

maximumPoolSizeMaximum connections per instance
minimumIdleWarm connections kept ready
idleTimeoutIdle connection eviction time
maxLifetimeMaximum lifetime before connection refresh

How to Size Your Pool Correctly

Use this simple guideline:

maximumPoolSize ≈ concurrent DB requests per instance

And always validate:

instances × maximumPoolSize < HANA DB connection limit

For example:

  • 3 instances
  • pool size 40
3 × 40 = 120 connections

Well within a 1000 connection limit.

Monitoring & Observability

You should continuously monitor:

  • Active DB connections
  • Idle connections
  • Pool utilization
  • Total HANA connection usage

Set alerts at 70–80% of DB capacity.

Common Pitfalls

  • Not enabling combinePools in multi-tenant apps
  • Oversizing connection pools
  • Multiple services sharing same HANA DB without coordination
  • No monitoring of DB connection usage
  • Assuming default values are safe for production

Summary

Area Recommendation

Connection PoolUse HikariCP
Multi-TenancyEnable combinePools
Pool SizingBased on concurrency
MonitoringMandatory
ScalingValidate against DB limits

Final Thoughts

Connection pooling is often invisible, until it fails in production.

With the right configuration in CAP Java and SAP HANA, you can:

  • avoid outages
  • improve performance
  • scale safely in multi-tenant environments

If you are running CAP Java in production, take a few minutes to review your pool configuration, it can prevent your next incident.

References

  • HikariCP
  • DB Connection Pooling
  • CAP Datasource Configuration

entry.title: Understanding SAP HANA Disaster Recovery - Technical Scope, Distance, and Data Protection Boundaries
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA provides documented mechanisms to support disaster recovery (DR) scenarios in which an entire site, data center, or availability zone becomes unavailable.

From a technical perspective, disaster recovery addresses failure domains that exceed the scope of local high-availability mechanisms. Its primary objective is to restore database operations after site-level outages, while preserving data consistency according to architecturally defined recovery objectives.

Disaster recovery is not a single feature. It is an architecture, built from documented mechanisms such as system replication, backup-based recovery, distance-aware configurations, and controlled takeover procedures.

This article consolidates SAP-documented SAP HANA disaster-recovery mechanisms into a single technical view.

Its purpose is to make scope, distance considerations, and data-protection boundaries explicit, supporting sound DR architecture design and precise technical discussions.

All statements are derived from official SAP Help Portal documentation.

1. Disaster Recovery in SAP HANA - Technical Definition
In SAP HANA documentation, disaster recovery refers to mechanisms that enable database availability after site-level failures, such as:

  • complete data-center outages
  • regional infrastructure failures
  • loss of connectivity between sites

Disaster recovery is explicitly distinct from:

  • High availability (HA)
    which minimizes downtime within a site or cluster
  • Backup and recovery
    which restore data to a previous state using backups and logs

This separation of objectives is consistently reflected in SAP HANA administration and architecture documentation.

Key boundary
Disaster recovery addresses failure domains, not individual components.

2. Failure Domains and Distance Considerations
A defining characteristic of disaster recovery is physical and logical separation between systems.

From a technical standpoint, DR architectures introduce:

  • network latency
  • increased failure isolation
  • asynchronous data-transfer requirements

Distance directly influences:

  • feasible replication modes
  • achievable Recovery Point Objective (RPO)
  • achievable Recovery Time Objective (RTO)

SAP HANA disaster-recovery mechanisms are therefore distance-aware by design.

Key boundary
Distance and latency are architectural constraints, not tuning parameters.

3. SAP HANA System Replication as a DR Mechanism
3.1 Technical Role in Disaster Recovery
SAP HANA System Replication (SR) is a primary building block for replication-based disaster recovery architectures.

In DR scenarios:

  • primary and secondary systems are deployed in separate sites
  • replication protects against site-level failures
  • takeover is initiated when the primary site is unavailable

System replication continuously transfers data changes from the primary system to the secondary system, maintaining a replica of the current database state.

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA System Replication - Overview

3.2 Replication Modes and Distance
SAP HANA system replication supports synchronous and asynchronous replication modes.
From a disaster-recovery perspective:

  • Synchronous replication
    is typically constrained by distance and latency and is therefore limited to shorter distances
  • Asynchronous replication
    is commonly used for long-distance DR scenarios and accepts some replication lag

The selected replication mode directly influences:

  • potential data loss during failover
  • achievable RPO values

This behavior is an inherent consequence of documented SR semantics.

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA System Replication - Replication Modes

Key boundary
Replication mode determines data-loss characteristics, not historical recovery capability.

4. Multi-Tier and Cascaded System Replication
SAP HANA supports multi-tier (cascaded) system replication, where data is replicated across more than one secondary system.

In disaster-recovery architectures, multi-tier replication can be used to:

  • combine high availability and disaster recovery objectives
  • separate near-site and far-site replicas
  • increase isolation between failure domains

Each replication tier operates within documented technical constraints, including supported replication paths and takeover rules.

SAP Help Portal reference
SAP HANA Multi-Tier System Replication
Supported Replication Modes Between Systems

Key boundary
Cascaded replication increases resilience, but does not replace backups.

5. Backup-Based Disaster Recovery
5.1 Technical Model
In addition to replication-based approaches, disaster recovery can be implemented using backup-based recovery.

In this model:

  • data and log backups are stored in a remote or isolated location
  • recovery is performed by restoring backups at the target site
  • point-in-time recovery is possible, depending on backup and log availability

Backup-based DR provides:

  • strong isolation between sites
  • access to historical restore points

It typically involves longer recovery times compared to replication-based DR.

SAP Help Portal reference
Planning Your Backup and Recovery Strategy

5.2 Replication-Based vs. Backup-Based DR
From a technical standpoint:

  • Replication-based DR
    prioritizes continuity with limited data loss
  • Backup-based DR
    prioritizes isolation and historical recovery

Most enterprise SAP HANA architectures combine both approaches to balance availability, data protection, and recovery flexibility.

6. Disaster Recovery vs. High Availability
Although both HA and DR may use system replication, their objectives and assumptions differ fundamentally.

  • High availability
    handles component-level failures within a site
  • Disaster recovery
    handles complete loss of a site or failure domain

Disaster recovery designs emphasize:

  • physical separation
  • controlled takeover procedures
  • explicitly defined data-loss boundaries

Key boundary
DR is validated against site-level failure scenarios, not host failures.

7. RPO and RTO in Disaster Recovery Context
In SAP HANA DR architectures:

  • RPO
    is influenced by replication mode or backup frequency
  • RTO
    is influenced by takeover procedures, recovery execution, and operational readiness

SAP HANA provides the technical mechanisms required to support different RPO/RTO targets.
Actual values are derived from architecture and configuration, not configured as product parameters.

8. Design Boundaries and Architectural Implications
From a disaster-recovery architecture perspective:

  • no single mechanism covers all site-level failure scenarios
  • replication-based DR trades isolation for continuity
  • backup-based DR trades speed for historical recovery
  • distance and latency impose hard technical constraints

Understanding these documented boundaries is essential for realistic DR expectations and technically sound architecture design.

Summary
SAP HANA provides documented technical mechanisms to support disaster-recovery scenarios involving site-level failures.

Disaster recovery is not defined by a single feature, but by the combination of system replication, backup mechanisms, distance-aware configuration, and controlled recovery procedures.

By understanding the documented scope, distance considerations, and data-protection boundaries of SAP HANA disaster-recovery mechanisms, architectures can be designed with clear, realistic, and technically defensible recovery expectations.

entry.title: BI Remediation Checks during ECC to S/4HANA Migration
entry.content:
As SAP prepares to end support for ECC by 2027, many organizations are making the crucial move to SAP S/4HANA. The shift can unlock improved capabilities and performance, but it can also introduce changes that ripple through the BI landscape if we don’t plan for them early.

From a BI perspective, there are multiple aspects to consider during the transition. This blog focuses on the BI remediation checks that help protect data trust, reporting, and decision-making continuity during and after the migration.

Why do we need BI remediation? 
The migration from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA introduces significant changes to the underlying data structures. These changes impact how data is extracted, modelled, and consumed in the BI system. Because of that, reporting remediation becomes critical to ensure data consistency, accuracy, and continuity in analytics after the migration.

With remediation, we ensure:

  • BI models and reports remain functional and aligned with the new S/4HANA structures
  • Disruption is minimized for business operations and reporting processes

Data model simplifications in S/4HANA (what changes underneath)
When SAP S/4HANA was first introduced, the SAP ERP 6.0 data model underwent major restructuring. Redundancies were removed, and many smaller tables were consolidated into fewer, larger ones.
Figure 1 can help visualize the concept and why “what worked in ECC” may not behave the same way in S/4HANA.

                                                                                                 FIGURE 1

Impact of S/4HANA migration on the BI system (what to check)

1) Impact on BW Datasources / Extractors
If you run SAP BW, one of the first questions is:
“After ECC becomes S/4HANA, will my extractors and DataSources still be supported and behave the same way?”
SAP note 2500202  provides an Excel sheet produced by SAP that details the impact of S/4HANA on BW Datasources. This sheet is periodically updated. The same note also contains a PPT that guides us on how to interpret and use the Excel list.

A proven approach is:

  • Inventory your active extractors (e.g., via ROOSOURCE).  
  • Identify which are truly used (process chains / BI statistics where available)
  • Cross-check them against the SAP note 2500202 list and categorize outcomes (no change / restricted / obsolete / alternative required)

2) Tables with Replacement Objects (don’t rely on “classic tables” blindly)
If, as part of your BI remediation strategy, you plan to continue using certain classic ECC tables, for example, relying on MARD instead of adopting the newer MATDOC structure, you must depend on the corresponding Replacement Object rather than accessing the table directly.
With the data model simplification in SAP S/4HANA, many traditional ECC tables are no longer the authoritative “single source of truth.” In many cases, data is aggregated or derived via CDS views that join and enrich information from multiple sources. Because of this shift, it becomes essential to transition from reading classic tables (such as MARC, MARD, and others) to using their designated Replacement Objects, ensuring that analytics and integrations receive accurate, up‑to‑date data.

  • In Native HANA, it is advisable to verify whether a table has a replacement object or whether it can still be used directly.
  • In SAP BW, the datasource typically absorbs most of the underlying data model changes. However, in hybrid scenarios, particularly when calculation views are built on ECC base tables or when custom datasources built on ECC base tables, it becomes essential to thoroughly assess the impact on the affected tables.

Figure 2 shows an example for table MARD. NSDM_E_MARD serves as the replacement view for the classic MARD table. Although data continues to reside in MARD, those entries generally represent older documents migrated to S/4HANA or new S/4HANA records that are stored without measure fields. To obtain the full set of information (both characteristics and measures) for transactions created in S/4HANA, you must use the replacement object, in this case NSDM_E_MARD, rather than relying on MARD directly.

                                                                                                     FIGURE 2

3) Replacement of obsolete tables
Some ECC tables are replaced or made obsolete, and direct reporting on such tables is no longer recommended or supported. Examples tables include:

  • KONV, VBUP, VBUK, EIKP, EIPO

Obsolete tables may still exist, but they can behave as compatibility views. For accuracy and performance, they should be switched to transparent tables where applicable.

  • SAP Note 3250204: describes about the replacement table PRDC_ELEMENTS for KNOV table.
  • SAP Note 2198647: talks about tables VBUP, VBUK and SD data model changes.
  • SAP Note 3482527: covers about new S/4HANA foreign trade. 

4) Key fields changed (primary key shifts can break pipelines)
In S/4HANA, the primary keys of some tables have changed compared to ECC. Some examples are:

  • VBFA
  • FAAV_ANLC

These changes directly affect how data is stored, identified, and retrieved. That can break:

  • Joins in HANA models
  • Deduplication logic
  • Delta logic or key-based transformations in BW

Action

  • Review where these tables are used in extraction/transformation layers
  • Re-check join conditions, uniqueness assumptions, and surrogate key logic
  • Re-test deltas and aggregations end-to-end

5) Field length extensions (small change, big downstream effect)
Some fields were extended in S/4HANA to support broader functional requirements, enhanced integration needs, and future extensibility.
One such example is:

  • VBAP-VBTYP (Char1) Document Category replaced by VBAP-VBTYPL (Char4)
    • SAP Note 2495386 details the BW datasource that uses this object.

Another major example:

  • Material Number (SAP Note 2215424) changed from char(18) to char(40)

A key design decision regarding material numbers is whether your organization will:

  • Adopt a true 40‑character material number, introducing a new business meaning, or
  • Retain the existing 18‑character logic, while simply expanding the technical field length to 40 characters.

The chosen approach directly influences how material numbers flow through your BI data models and determines the adjustments required in downstream reporting and integrations.

6) Centralized Master Data via Business Partner (Customer/Vendor integration)
SAP fundamentally changed how master data is managed by introducing a unified, centralized model for customers, vendors, and business partners. This replaces the traditional ECC siloed structures with a harmonized object: the Business Partner (BP).
It’s not a “technical-only” change, it’s a semantic change in how master data is governed
Please check the SAP Note 2265093 note for more information.

7) Redirecting ECC Virtual Tables in HANA Calculation Views
When your BI models use calculation views that reference ECC base tables, these views must be updated after the migration to ensure they point to the corresponding objects in the S/4HANA system. Redirecting them is essential to prevent calculation view failures and to avoid inconsistent or incorrect analytical results after the migration. Please refer the blog for mitigation of this impact.

 
Final thoughts: ECC to S/4HANA migration is not only an ERP transformation, but also a BI continuity challenge. The safest approach is to treat BI remediation as a structured workstream, validating extractors, replacing obsolete dependencies, and proactively addressing table/key/field changes before they surface as production reporting issues.

If this post helps you structure your BI remediation checks, please consider sharing your own experiences or additions in the comments.

entry.title: Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY |  TECHNICAL GUIDE  |  SAP HANA CLOUD |  PART 1 OF 2

Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better

Part 1 of 2: The genuine capabilities that open up when you move from on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud — HDI containers, SAP AI Core, and the BTP application ecosystem.

By Deepa Dorairaj   | SAP AI Solution Architect  |  HANA Data Engineering  |  Published 2026

This is Part 1 of a two-part series on SAP HANA Cloud migration. Part 1 covers what genuinely improves when you move to the cloud. Part 2 covers the pain points and what to watch out for. Both perspectives are necessary for an honest picture of the migration journey.

TL;DR: Moving from on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud is not just an infrastructure change. It unlocks three capabilities that are difficult or impossible to replicate on-premise: a modern database development workflow through HDI containers and Git, access to SAP AI Core for production-grade AI deployments on your SAP data, and a rich application ecosystem through BTP that connects your HANA data to S/4HANA, Fiori, Integration Suite, and beyond.

Why This Article Exists

Most migration content falls into one of two traps: vendor-produced material that glosses over the real challenges, or war stories that focus exclusively on what broke. The honest picture is more nuanced than either.

Part 2 of this series covers the friction — the tooling gaps, scripting compatibility issues, and security model changes that will slow your team down. You should read that too, especially if you are in the planning phase.

But this article is about the other side of that equation: what genuinely gets better. Not what SAP's marketing says gets better — what actually changes for the teams doing real work in real HANA Cloud environments. The three areas covered here represent capabilities that were either unavailable, severely limited, or operationally painful in on-premise. In HANA Cloud they become first-class, production-ready patterns.

IMPROVEMENT 1 OF 3 

HDI Containers: A Modern Database Development Workflow

What HDI Actually Is — and Why It Changes Everything

The HANA Deployment Infrastructure (HDI) container model is the single biggest workflow improvement that HANA Cloud brings for development teams. On-premise HANA, most teams deploy database objects directly against schemas — DDL scripts run manually or through transport management, objects exist in a shared schema that everyone touches, and version control is an afterthought bolted on top of an inherently stateful system.

HDI flips this model entirely. Database objects are defined as source artifacts — .hdbtable, .hdbview, .hdbprocedure files — that live in a Git repository alongside application code. The HDI container is deployed from those artifacts, and the deployment engine handles dependency resolution, delta deployment, and environment isolation automatically.

The result is that your database development workflow starts to look like your application development workflow: version controlled, reviewable, deployable, and reproducible.

Git Integration: Version Control as a First-Class Citizen

On-premise HANA development rarely had true version control for database objects. Teams used SAP Transport Management for moving objects between landscapes, but transport management is not version control — it doesn't give you branching, pull requests, history, or the ability to roll back to any point in time with confidence.

HANA Cloud's HDI model integrates directly with Git repositories through SAP Business Application Studio and the HANA Cloud toolchain. Every database object is a file in a repository. Changes go through the same review process as application code. Deployments are triggered from specific commits or branches.

  • Feature branches for database changes — developers work on isolated branches without affecting the shared schema, exactly as they would for application code.
  • Pull request reviews for schema changes — DDL changes are reviewed by peers before deployment, catching errors before they reach development or production environments.
  • Full commit history for every database object — the ability to see exactly what changed, when, and who made the change, with the ability to revert to any previous state.
  • Branch-based environment promotion — development, QA, and production environments are deployed from specific branches, making the promotion process auditable and repeatable.

This is the change that development teams feel most immediately after migration. The frustration of untraceable schema changes, overwritten objects, and transport conflicts disappears when database artifacts live in Git like everything else.

Environment Isolation and Team Collaboration

On-premise HANA landscapes typically have a development system, a QA system, and a production system — three full HANA instances that are expensive to maintain and still don't provide true isolation between individual developers working simultaneously.

HDI containers provide lightweight, isolated development environments within a single HANA Cloud instance. Each developer or team can have their own container — a complete, isolated copy of the database schema — without requiring a separate HANA system. Changes in one container have zero impact on another.

  • Developer containers — each developer works in their own isolated container, testing changes without risk of interfering with colleagues.
  • Feature containers — a complete isolated environment for a specific feature or project, spun up and torn down as needed.
  • Automated dependency management — the HDI deployment engine resolves object dependencies automatically. If a view depends on a table that doesn't exist yet, the deployment fails with a clear error rather than creating an inconsistent state silently.
  • Simplified landscape transport — promoting changes from development to QA to production means deploying the same Git artifacts to different containers, not managing SAP transport requests.

Cleaner Deployment and Versioning

One of the most underappreciated HDI benefits is idempotent deployment. On-premise, re-running a DDL script against an existing object requires DROP and recreate logic, ALTER statements, or manual intervention. HDI's deployment engine compares the desired state (your artifact files) against the current state (what's in the container) and applies only the necessary changes — automatically, safely, and repeatably.

-- On-premise: manual drop and recreate required

DROP TABLE my_schema.my_table;

CREATE TABLE my_schema.my_table (id INT, name VARCHAR(100));

-- HDI: define once in .hdbtable artifact, deploy repeatedly

-- HDI engine handles delta — no manual DROP/CREATE needed

COLUMN TABLE my_table (id INT, name NVARCHAR(100));

 

Capability

What It Unlocks

Git version control

Full history, branching, pull request reviews, and rollback for every database object

Developer isolation

Individual HDI containers eliminate shared schema conflicts between developers

Automated dependency resolution

HDI deployment engine manages object dependencies — no manual sequencing of DDL scripts

Idempotent deployment

Deploy the same artifacts repeatedly without manual DROP/CREATE logic

Landscape transport

Git-based promotion replaces SAP transport requests for database objects

Feature branching

Database changes developed and tested in isolation before merging to main branch

IMPROVEMENT 2 OF 3 

SAP AI Core: Production-Grade AI on Your SAP Data

Why AI Core Changes the Enterprise AI Equation

On-premise HANA has machine learning capabilities — PAL (Predictive Analysis Library) and APL (Automated Predictive Library) provide a solid foundation for classical ML workloads that run close to the data. But these capabilities have a fundamental limitation: they are designed for traditional ML patterns and do not provide a path to deploying modern foundation models, RAG systems, or generative AI against your SAP data in a production-ready, governed way.

SAP AI Core, accessible through BTP and natively integrated with HANA Cloud, is a different category of capability entirely. It is a managed AI runtime that handles the infrastructure, scaling, and lifecycle management of AI workloads — allowing you to focus on the AI logic rather than the infrastructure that runs it.

For SAP architects and data engineers, the critical insight is this: AI Core is where your HANA Cloud data becomes the foundation for genuinely intelligent enterprise applications, not just analytical ones.

Vector Embeddings and RAG for SAP Data

HANA Cloud includes native vector storage capabilities — the ability to store and query vector embeddings directly in the database alongside your structured SAP data. This is the foundational capability for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems that can answer questions about your SAP landscape using live data rather than static training.

The architecture this enables is significant: instead of sending sensitive SAP business data to an external AI service for every query, you store embeddings of your SAP content in HANA Cloud's vector store and retrieve only the relevant context for each query. The foundation model gets the context it needs without your raw SAP data leaving your controlled environment.

-- Create a vector store table in HANA Cloud

CREATE TABLE sap_knowledge_embeddings (

  id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,

  content     NCLOB,

  source_type NVARCHAR(100),

  embedding   REAL_VECTOR(1536)

);

-- Semantic similarity search against SAP knowledge base

SELECT TOP 5 id, content, source_type,

  COSINE_SIMILARITY(embedding, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS score

FROM sap_knowledge_embeddings

ORDER BY score DESC;

This pattern — storing SAP process documentation, BAPI descriptions, master data definitions, and business rules as searchable embeddings — is the foundation of a genuine enterprise AI assistant that knows your specific SAP landscape, not just generic SAP knowledge.

Connecting Foundation Models via AI Core APIs

AI Core provides governed, auditable access to foundation models — including SAP's own generative AI models and third-party models through the Generative AI Hub — with the enterprise controls that on-premise environments cannot provide: usage tracking, cost allocation, rate limiting, and compliance logging built in.

The connection between HANA Cloud data and AI Core models enables patterns that were genuinely not achievable on-premise:

  • Intelligent document processing — extracting structured data from unstructured SAP documents (purchase orders, invoices, contracts) using foundation models and storing results directly in HANA Cloud tables.
  • Natural language interfaces to SAP data — allowing business users to query HANA Cloud analytics in plain language, with AI Core translating natural language to SQL and grounding responses in live data.
  • Automated exception handling — AI agents that monitor SAP business processes, identify anomalies in HANA Cloud data, and take corrective actions through SAP APIs without human intervention.
  • Fine-tuned models on SAP data — training or fine-tuning foundation models on your organization's specific SAP data patterns through AI Core's managed training infrastructure.

Grounding AI in Live SAP Business Data

The most powerful aspect of the HANA Cloud and AI Core combination is the ability to ground AI outputs in live, governed SAP business data. Generic AI models hallucinate when asked about your specific business — your customer master, your material catalog, your financial postings. Grounded models, retrieval systems, and tool-calling agents that query HANA Cloud directly produce outputs that are accurate, current, and auditable.

The architecture pattern that is emerging as the standard for enterprise SAP AI: HANA Cloud as the data foundation and vector store → AI Core as the model runtime and orchestration layer → SAP Business AI applications as the user-facing interface. Each layer does what it is best at, and the combination is genuinely more capable than any of its parts.

Capability

What It Unlocks

Native vector storage

Store and query embeddings directly in HANA Cloud alongside structured SAP data

RAG on SAP data

Build retrieval systems that answer questions using live SAP content without external data exposure

Foundation model access

Governed, auditable access to SAP and third-party foundation models through Generative AI Hub

Fine-tuning capability

Train models on your specific SAP data patterns through AI Core's managed infrastructure

Grounded AI outputs

AI responses anchored to live HANA Cloud data — accurate, current, and auditable

Intelligent automation

AI agents that monitor SAP processes and act on HANA data without human intervention

IMPROVEMENT 3 OF 3 

The BTP Application Ecosystem: Connecting HANA Cloud to Everything

From Database to Platform

On-premise HANA is primarily a database — an exceptional one, but a database. Applications connect to it, consume its data, and leave. The integration patterns are well established but fundamentally passive: HANA stores and processes data, applications request it.

HANA Cloud on BTP is part of a platform ecosystem that changes this dynamic. The connections between HANA Cloud and the broader SAP and non-SAP landscape are richer, more standardized, and more capable than what on-premise environments support. Your HANA Cloud instance is not just a database — it is a data foundation for a platform that can build, connect, and deploy enterprise applications at a speed that on-premise infrastructure cannot match.

Connecting HANA Cloud to S/4HANA and ECC

The integration between HANA Cloud and SAP's core ERP systems — S/4HANA and ECC — is one of the most practically valuable improvements for organizations running mixed landscapes. On-premise, cross-system data access required custom RFC connections, ALE configurations, or ETL pipelines that were expensive to build and fragile to maintain.

Through BTP's Integration Suite and HANA Cloud's federation capabilities, S/4HANA and ECC data becomes accessible in HANA Cloud with significantly less custom integration work. This enables analytical and AI use cases that span transactional ERP data and the broader data landscape without requiring full data replication.

  • Real-time operational reporting on S/4HANA data through HANA Cloud without impacting ERP system performance.
  • Cross-system analytics combining ERP data with data from non-SAP sources in a single HANA Cloud analytical layer.
  • AI and ML workloads that consume live ERP data through governed, auditable connections rather than stale data extracts.

Building Fiori and BTP Applications on HANA Cloud Data

SAP Business Application Studio — the cloud-based IDE that replaces the Eclipse-based tooling of on-premise development — provides a first-class development environment for building Fiori applications, CAP (Cloud Application Programming model) services, and full-stack BTP applications directly on top of HANA Cloud data.

The CAP model in particular represents a significant improvement over traditional on-premise application development patterns. CAP services expose HANA Cloud data through OData and REST APIs with built-in authorization, query handling, and SAP Fiori integration — dramatically reducing the boilerplate code required to build enterprise-grade applications on SAP data.

-- CAP service definition exposing HANA Cloud entity

service AnalyticsService {

  @readonly entity SalesOrders as

    SELECT from db.SalesOrders {

      order_id, customer, amount, status,

      created_at, region

    } where status = 'ACTIVE';

}

-- Automatically generates OData V4 endpoint

-- with filtering, sorting, and pagination built in

OData and REST API Exposure

Exposing HANA Cloud data through standardized OData and REST APIs — consumable by Fiori applications, third-party tools, mobile applications, and external systems — is significantly more streamlined on BTP than on-premise. The CAP framework handles the API layer, and BTP's API Management provides governance, rate limiting, and monitoring for those APIs across all consumers.

For organizations building an enterprise data mesh or API-first data strategy, this is one of the most compelling HANA Cloud advantages — your HANA data becomes a governed, discoverable, consumable API product rather than a database that specific applications happen to know how to query.

SAP Integration Suite and Side-by-Side Extensibility

SAP Integration Suite on BTP provides pre-built integration flows between HANA Cloud and the broader SAP and non-SAP ecosystem — Salesforce, Microsoft, ServiceNow, and hundreds of other systems — without custom middleware development. On-premise integration required custom development, dedicated middleware servers, and ongoing maintenance of bespoke integration code.

Side-by-side extensibility — the BTP pattern for extending S/4HANA and other SAP systems without modifying core — relies on HANA Cloud as its data persistence layer. Extension applications built on BTP consume S/4HANA data through governed APIs, process and store results in HANA Cloud, and surface insights back to SAP users through Fiori — all without touching the core ERP system.

Side-by-side extensibility is the pattern that makes HANA Cloud most valuable for organizations that need to innovate on top of S/4HANA without the risk and cost of core modifications. HANA Cloud is the data foundation that makes this pattern production-ready.

Capability

What It Unlocks

S/4HANA and ECC integration

Cross-system analytics and AI workloads on live ERP data without custom RFC development

CAP application development

Build Fiori and full-stack BTP applications on HANA Cloud data with dramatically less boilerplate

OData and REST APIs

Expose HANA Cloud data as governed, discoverable API products consumable by any client

SAP Integration Suite

Pre-built integration flows to SAP and non-SAP systems without custom middleware

Side-by-side extensibility

Extend S/4HANA through BTP applications backed by HANA Cloud without core modifications

BTP ecosystem

Access to the full BTP service catalog — AI, analytics, integration, automation — from a single platform

The Honest Summary

The three improvements covered in this article are not incremental upgrades to what on-premise HANA already does. They are qualitatively different capabilities that change what is possible for development teams, data engineers, and enterprise architects working with SAP data.

HDI containers with Git integration bring database development into the modern software development workflow — version controlled, isolated, reviewable, and reproducible. SAP AI Core unlocks production-grade AI on your SAP data — RAG systems, foundation model access, intelligent automation — that simply cannot be built with the same reliability and governance on-premise. And the BTP application ecosystem transforms HANA Cloud from a database into a platform foundation for the full range of SAP and cross-system enterprise applications.

None of this makes the migration easy. Part 2 of this series is honest about the friction — and there is real friction. But the capabilities waiting on the other side of that friction are substantial enough to make the journey worth taking.

Part 2 of this series covers the pain points in detail: HANA Studio to Cloud Central tooling gaps, stored procedure compatibility issues, and security model changes that will catch your team off guard. Read both before finalizing your migration plan.

entry.title: Good to know: "HASH Partitioning" vs "RANGE Partitioning" in context of SAP HANA database
entry.content:
• Why was this blog post created?
  • What are the differences?
  • Where can I find the most important information about the topic of SAP HANA partitioning?
  • Complexity
  • What is the conclusion?
  • Other articles
  • Do you have further questions?
  • Contribution

 

last updated: 2026-03-04

Why was this blog post created?

The area of partitioning is a very complex topic when it comes to the SAP HANA database (in case of all kind of database). The blog post aim is to summarize the facts and explain the different types on high level (pro, cons). Deeper understanding is available in the referenced documents. A high-level understanding is a critical step in order to choose the correct and most effective partitioning in your actual need/situation.

What are the differences?

The following tables contains the differences. Please note that this is a general categorization, both HASH and RANGE type of partitioning method are more complex and intricate than this table mentions. The table uses a simplified categorization.

 HASHRANGE
Amount of dataTypically less data.Typically huge amount of data.
When to use?Solves the 2 billion limit.Next to partitioning (2 billion limit) there are further requirements (e.g. performance needs).
FocusFocus is on the partitioning itself.Focus is not only the partitioning but further requirements also.
ImplementationEasier setup.Preparation and planning necessary, more complex task.
Implementation TypeMainly technical task. Planning is also necessary and depending on selectivity.More deeper and detailed planning & technical tasks.
Example3281773 - What cause the non uniform data distribution in HASH partitioned table in SAP HANA?2289491 - Best Practices for Partitioning of Finance Tables
InvolvementMainly a DBA task but data owner participation can be required.Need further teams (e.g. application knowledge).

 

Where can I find the most important information about the topic of SAP HANA partitioning?

SAP Knowledge Base Article(s):

  • 2044468 - FAQ: SAP HANA Partitioning
  • 3146645 - What is the best approach in partitioning tables on SAP HANA?
  • 3307500 - How to decide which partitioning type and column(s) should been used to partition a table in SAP HANA?

SAP Community Article:

  • Collected information regarding partitioning in SAP HANA (with examples)

Complexity

The complexity is also indicated by the fact that all relevant information can be found in the central documentation (2044468), however, based on experience, further explanations are needed for the interpretation and use of this documentation, which is why additional supporting documents were created (e.g. 3146645 or 3307500). Furthermore, the Blog listed above was created to present specific examples and to compile the supporting documents.

What is the conclusion?

Understanding is the key element to do the proper partitioning of the table based on different requirements. This document explains a top down approach and gives an overview about the topic and the necessary documentations.

Other articles

✍️ Where can I find knowledge and information belongs to SAP HANA?
✍️ Where can I find information about the available tools for SAP HANA (all types of use)?

Do you have further questions?

Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have question or observation regarding the article.
Q&A link for SAP HANA: https://answers.sap.com/tags/73554900100700000996 

Contribution

If you find any missing information belongs to the topic, please let me know. I am happy to add the new content. My intention is to maintain the content continuously to keep the info up-to-date.

Release Information

Release DateDescription
2026.03.04First/initial Release of the SAP Blog Post documentation (Technical Article).

entry.title: Enterprise AI in Action: SAP Joule Delivers Where Others Don’t
entry.content:
AI is becoming part of everyday enterprise work, from drafting emails to designing complex processes. While ChatGPT and Gemini can generate functional specs and ABAP code, the real question is: “Why use SAP Joule?”
The difference is context – Joule is embedded in S/4HANA, understanding system configuration, business rules, workflows, and live data to deliver outputs that are accurate, compliant, and ready for real-world use. Unlike general-purpose AI, it doesn’t just produce well-written text; it grasps the technical and business landscape to ensure every recommendation aligns with your enterprise environment.
To understand this difference in practical terms, let’s look at two common S/4HANA scenarios: a Purchase Order compliance check and a Sales Order credit validation. On the surface, both appear straightforward – define the requirement, identify the enhancement spot, document the logic, and generate the necessary ABAP code.
A general-purpose AI tool can certainly help structure the functional specification and outline the technical approach. However, the real challenge in enterprise projects is not drafting the document – it is ensuring that the design aligns with the system’s actual configuration, enhancement framework, workflow setup, authorization model, and business data. This is where the distinction between generic AI and embedded, SAP-native intelligence becomes clear.

1. Purchase Order Compliance Check
Business Requirement: Block Purchase Order creation if:
• Vendor is not approved in a custom Z-table
• PO value exceeds 1,000,000
• Purchasing group = “A01”
• Mandatory attachment is missing
Using Chatgpt or Gemini:
• Enhancement via BAdI ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST
• Logic to check vendor approval, PO value, purchasing group, and attachment
• Error messages and test cases
Pros: Clear FS structure, general logic explained
Cons: Generic — it won’t know your actual Z-table structure, workflow configuration, or document type setup. You must feed these details manually.
Using SAP Joule:
• Identifies the correct BAdI or workflow in your S/4 system
• Pulls actual vendor master and custom table fields
• Checks your approval strategy and document type configuration
• Generates FS logic aligned with your live system setup
Pros: System-grounded, accurate, reduces rework, supports compliance

2. Sales Order Credit Validation
Business Requirement: Prevent Sales Order creation if a customer’s credit limit is exceeded.
Using Chatgpt or Gemini:
• Identify a relevant BAdI or user exit
• Check credit exposure against the limit
• Trigger an error message if the limit is exceeded
• Suggest test cases for validation
Pros: Clean structure, well-written, and easy to understand
Cons: Generic — it doesn’t know your actual credit control area, company code setup, or the exact fields and BAdIs in your S/4HANA system. All system-specific details must be manually added.
Using SAP Joule:
• Detect the active BAdI or enhancement spot in your system
• Pull the correct credit control configuration and field names
• Align logic with company code settings and authorization roles
• Suggest tests based on your live system data
Pros: Context-aware, system-aligned, lower risk of errors

Key Differentiators

  1. Verified SAP Knowledge Base: Responses are grounded in SAP's official documentation and verified sources.
  2. SAP-Specific Methodology: Understands SAP implementation approaches, configuration requirements, and technical constraints.
  3. Industry-Specific Compliance: Aligned with SAP's consulting standards and best practices.
  4. SAP Ecosystem Integration: Deep understanding of how SAP modules, technologies, and processes interconnect.

Comparison Table

Capability

SAP Joule for Consultants

ChatGPT

Gemini

Notes

SAP Domain Expertise

Deep, verified SAP knowledge from official sources

General SAP knowledge from public training data

General SAP knowledge with real-time search

Joule is trained on SAP-verified documentation

Template Generation

SAP-specific templates and methodologies

Generic business templates

Generic business templates with Google integration

Templates follow SAP best practices and standards

Functional Specification Writing

SAP implementation-focused with proper technical structure [3],[4]

General functional spec approaches

Structured but generic approaches

Joule understands SAP-specific requirements and constraints

Industry Standards Compliance

SAP best practices and implementation standards

General industry standards

General standards with research capabilities

Aligned with SAP's consulting methodologies

Technical Documentation Structure

SAP-aligned formats (ABAP, configuration paths, transaction codes) [2],[5]

Generic technical documentation

Structured technical docs

Joule provide SAP-specific technical details and paths

Integration Knowledge

Deep SAP ecosystem and module integration expertise

Broad but surface-level SAP integration knowledge

General integration knowledge

Joule understand SAP module interdependencies and best practices

Business Process Understanding

SAP module-specific processes and workflows

General business process knowledge

General business knowledge with research

Joule trained on SAP business process documentation

For functional specification writing in SAP contexts, Joule is the superior choice because:

  • Accuracy: knowledge comes from verified SAP sources rather than general internet training data. 
  • Relevance: understands SAP-specific technical requirements, configuration paths, and implementation constraints. 
  • Structure: can provide proper SAP documentation formats, including transaction codes, customization paths, and technical specifications. 
  • Best Practices: aligned with SAP's consulting methodologies and implementation standards.

We’ve seen how generic AI can draft specs and code, but context matters. In your S/4HANA projects, where has AI helped — and where has it fallen short? Share your experiences, challenges, or tips below!

Source:

SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help

Joule

Other Blogs:

From Conversational AI to Business AI: Why SAP Jou... - SAP Community

System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA - SAP Community
